evidence.title: Management of Sore Throat: Time to Update | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Because rheumatic heart disease is so rare in the United States, the primary benefit of using antibiotics is symptom reduction, with a number needed to treat of 6 for improvement by day 3, and possibly the reduction of spread to others (although most close contacts have likely already been exposed by the time the patient is evaluated). 15 , 16 In 2023, an international group of experts in primary care respiratory infections published the first international guideline for the management of sore throat . 3 The guideline focuses on avoiding testing and antibiotic use for patients with uncomplicated sore throat (i.e., mild to moderate sore throat accompanied by cough with no red flags), reserving evaluation, testing, and possibly antibiotic use for those at risk of complicated or severe disease. 3 Using rapid antigen testing for streptococcus, followed by prescribing antibiotics, is not reco

evidence.title: Clinical Guidance for Group A Streptococcal Pharyngitis | Group A Strep | CDC
evidence.snippet: Cephalexin, oral 20 mg/kg/dose twice daily (maximum = 500 mg/dose) for 10 days Cefadroxil, oral 30 mg/kg once daily (maximum = 1 g) for 10 days Clindamycin, oral 7 mg/kg/dose 3 times daily (maximum = 300 mg/dose) for 10 days Azithromycin, oral 12 mg/kg once (maximum = 500 mg), then 6 mg/kg (max=250 mg) once daily for the next 4 days Clarithromycin, oral 7.5 mg/kg/dose twice daily (maximum = 250 mg/dose) for 10 days Antibiotic resistance There's never been a report of a clinical isolate of group A strep bacteria that's resistant to penicillin or cephalosporins.

evidence.title: Strep A Test: MedlinePlus Medical Test
evidence.snippet: If you or your child is diagnosed with strep throat, you will need to take antibiotics. Most often you will need to take them for 10 days. After a day or two of taking the medicine, you or your child should start to feel better. Most people are no longer contagious after taking antibiotics for 24 hours. But it's important to take all the medicine as prescribed. Stopping early can lead to serious complications, such as:

note_text:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with a 2-day history of sore throat accompanied by fever, chills, and swollen glands. She denies cough, nasal congestion, difficulty swallowing, rash, difficulty breathing, chest pain, drooling, or difficulty opening her mouth. She reports recent exposure to strep throat from her son last week. She has not taken any medications except supportive care with tea and honey. She has a known allergy to penicillin.

O: Phone encounter only; no physical examination or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is acute bacterial pharyngitis, likely streptococcal given the exposure history, fever, and swollen glands.
Differential:
1. Streptococcal pharyngitis
2. Viral pharyngitis
3. Infectious mononucleosis
4. Other bacterial pharyngitis

P: - First-line medication: Azithromycin 500 mg orally on day 1, then 250 mg orally once daily on days 2 through 5 (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Alternatives: Clindamycin 300 mg orally three times daily for 10 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Medication safety considerations: Avoid penicillin due to allergy; monitor for gastrointestinal side effects with macrolides or clindamycin; other contraindications not assessed in this telephone encounter.
- Follow-up recommendations: Seek in-person medical care urgently if difficulty breathing, severe throat pain, inability to swallow, drooling, or worsening symptoms occur; seek emergency care if signs of airway obstruction or severe systemic illness develop.
- Testing: Arrange for throat swab rapid antigen detection test or throat culture to confirm streptococcal infection if feasible.
